title: "Permission denied" error when running parallel containers that mount and run a single script
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I build a container image and use it as an environment for running tests.
The tests are run in parallel according to the number of CPU cores.
The tests are executed via a script that exists in a working directory and is mounted into the containers.
Running the containers in parallel fails on permission errors. This is related to the shared script that is mounted to all containers.
There is probably some race condition, because adding a sleep fixes the problem (the running containers still run, the sleep doesn't make them run in sequence).


**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. echo "#!/bin/sh
date
sleep 5" > script.sh

2. chmod a+x script.sh

3. write the following script and run it:
```
#!/bin/sh

for i in $(seq 3); do
    # the following sleep usually makes the parallel runs work
    # a longer sleep (0.5s) works better
    # sleep 0.1
    podman run \
    --volume .:/root/workdir:rw,Z \
    --workdir /root/workdir \
    -it fedora:33 \
    ./script.sh &
done
```

**Describe the results you received:**
/bin/sh: /root/workdir/./script.sh: Permission denied

**Describe the results you expected:**
dates printed


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.0.0-dev
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.6
Built:        Wed Dec 16 00:37:59 2020
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


comments.body: I guess this is blocked by selinux. You have to use a lowercase `z` for the volume mount options. see:https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/docs/source/markdown/podman-run.1.md#--volume--vsource-volumehost-dircontainer-diroptions

Thanks for pointing to the `z` option. It seems to be working fine.
I'm not sure why the `Z` option behaves differently depending on if I use sleep or not.
My expectation is that that podman/overlayfs should always pass or report an error, but maybe I simply don't understand how it works internally and this is expected.

Use :z rather then :Z if you want the content to be shared between containers.  But a better case would be to change your script to do

chcon -t container_file_t -l s0 -R .

And then don't do the :Z or :z 

Otherwise every container will run the equivalent of chcon command above.  Rather then once.

> My expectation is that that podman/overlayfs should always pass or report an error, but maybe I simply don't understand how it works internally and this is expected.

Well that's a classic race condition. Before the container is executed the volume is relabelled. With `Z` only the current container is allowed to access the files. If you run this in parallel the second `podman run` call might have relabelled the directory before the first container executes the script.

@rhatdan wrote a great blog on this topic that I consult frequently when facing selinux mount issues:

https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/user-namespaces-selinux-rootless-containers
